Another accidental commit triggered the error.

The commit needs to be undone again. The root cause is a symlink mismatch: `.cursor/skills/impeccable` was replaced by a symlink pointing outside the repository, but Git still tracks the old real file paths. This causes `git add` or a pre-commit hook to fail when processing those paths.

Two fixes are being weighed. The preferred option is to remove the symlinked directory from Git entirely, adding it to `.gitignore` and unstaging the cached files. The alternative keeps the symlink but requires careful staging of deletions and the symlink itself. The next step is to undo the recent commit and provide clear instructions.

The committed symlink points to an absolute path outside the repository. That path will not exist on other machines or CI. The best fix is to stop tracking `.cursor/skills/impeccable` entirely. Add that directory to `.gitignore` and remove its cached entries from Git.

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
The error indicates `.cursor/skills/impeccable/reference/adapt.md` is beyond a symbolic link, preventing Git from processing the path during commit.

This likely comes from `git add` or the IDE staging a file inside a symlinked directory. The `impeccible` reference was switched from vendored files to a symlink via Impeccable sync. Git cannot stage individual files under a symlink. The user needs to stage the symlink itself or adjust their workflow.

The recommended fix is to ignore the symlinked directory entirely. Add `.cursor/skills/impeccable` to `.gitignore`, then run `git rm -r --cached .cursor/skills/impeccable`. This removes all tracked files from the index while leaving the symlink on disk. Other changes can then be committed normally.
</details></think>

_**Agent (default)**_

**What happened:** Impeccable sync replaced the vendored `.cursor/skills/impeccable/` directory with a symlink to your local checkout:

```
.cursor/skills/impeccable → ../../../impeccable/dist/universal/.cursor/skills/impeccable
```

Git still had ~94 real files tracked under that path. When you run `git add` (or your editor stages files individually), Git tries to follow paths like `.cursor/skills/impeccable/reference/adapt.md` through the symlink and refuses — it won't traverse paths beyond a symlink boundary.

**What I fixed:**

1. Added `.cursor/skills/impeccable` to `.gitignore` — it’s meant to be linked locally per your [sync-upstreams skill](.cursor/skills/sync-upstreams/SKILL.md), not vendored in the repo.
2. Removed impeccable from the Git index with `git rm -r --cached .cursor/skills/impeccable` — this stages deletion of the old vendored files without touching the symlink on disk.

Your symlink still works locally. `git add -A` and `git commit` should succeed now.

**If you hit this again on another machine**, the one-liner is:

```bash
git rm -r --cached .cursor/skills/impeccable
```

Then make sure `.cursor/skills/impeccable` is in `.gitignore` before running `git add -A`.

I did **not** create a commit — your other staged changes are ready whenever you want to commit. The impeccable deletions and `.gitignore` update will be included in that commit.

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
There is extensive history in .specstory about this exact issue.

The issues page uses `collect-note-issues.html`, while rendered pages use `render-content.html`. Broken wikilinks are visible on rendered pages but missing from the issues page. This suggests a discrepancy between the two pipelines.

`collect-note-issues.html` scans RawContent for `[[...]]` patterns. Broken links visible on rendered pages must be absent from RawContent. They may have been injected by shortcodes or relationship lines rather than existing in the source files.

Broken wikilinks likely appear in frontmatter relationship lines or dynamic partials such as see-also, which are rendered but not scanned by the issues collector. The issues page may also be using stale cached partials. A regex mismatch between the lint script and the collection logic could explain why some broken links survive the replacement pass. Next steps include inspecting the see-also template, relationship line generation, and running a full Hugo build to compare outputs.
</details></think>

Broken wikilinks exist in the built HTML, yet the issues page shows no issues. Only the "Missing note" description text appears.

The root cause likely stems from a mismatch between data sources. render-content processes rendered HTML, while collect-note-issues reads raw markdown. Broken wikilinks may therefore exist in the rendered output but not in the raw content.

Hugo likely leaves `[[wikilink]]` syntax untouched in rendered HTML. The collect-note-issues regex is stricter than render-content's replacement pattern, so it may miss certain link formats.

Specific edge cases could explain the gap. The collect regex might fail on wikilinks containing pipes, such as `[[Note|with|pipes]]`. Broken links might also originate from shortcodes that emit `[[...]]` in rendered HTML without appearing in raw content. I will inspect the built HTML to identify exact broken link patterns and compare them against the collection logic.

A specific example is "Note Relationships". If the registry had this entry, render-content would link it instead of leaving it broken. The broken appearance confirms the registry lacks the key at render time. This points to a registry lookup failure rather than a regex mismatch.

Both paths cache the same registry partial, so a stale cache is unlikely. The real mismatch may be case sensitivity. Render-content replaces wikilinks with exact titles, but the registry lowercases keys for lookup. A title case difference could break the replacement even when the note exists.
</details></think>

The issues page reports no link issues found, yet the [[Cards]] wiki link should be broken.

Hugo lint also misses broken wikilinks. The registry matches `[[Cards]]` against the lowercase ContentBaseName "cards", so collect-note-issues treats it as valid. The template renders it as `[[Flashcards]]`.

The template renders `[[Cards]]` unchanged because slug replacement uses lowercase "cards". This creates three mismatches: registry lookup is case-insensitive while rendering is case-sensitive; self-links are skipped in render but treated as valid in the registry; and code spans are stripped during lint but preserved in render. The fix should align both systems.

The template should use the resolve-target partial instead of brute-force replacement. Self-links should render as plain text or styled anchors rather than broken links. The registry treats case-insensitive matches as valid, so the real problem is broken rendering.

The user likely sees genuinely missing notes, not false positives. The `[[Cards]]` case-sensitivity bug is a real rendering issue. The template must skip `<code>` blocks during wikilink replacement. Both render and collection logic need alignment so the issues page accurately reflects broken links.

The template should switch from a per-note replace loop to a single regex pass over all `[[...]]` wikilinks. Each match would resolve case-insensitively through the resolve-target partial, skipping content inside `<code>`, `<pre>`, and existing `<a>` tags. Links that resolve case-insensitively in the registry should not appear on the issues page. Self-links and code examples are not broken and should be excluded from reports.

    m = re.search(r&#39;^title:\s*\&#34;([^\&#34;]+)\&#34;&#39;, text, re.M)
    if not m: continue
    title = m.group(1)
    slug = re.search(r&#39;^slug:\s*\&#34;([^\&#34;]+)\&#34;&#39;, text, re.M)
    slug = slug.group(1) if slug else p.stem
    aliases = re.findall(r&#39;aliases:\s*\[([^\]]+)\]&#39;, text, re.M)
    keys = [title, slug, p.stem]
    for a in aliases:
        keys += [x.strip().strip(&#39;\&#34;&#39;) for x in a.group(1).split(&#39;,&#39;)]
    for k in keys:
        registry[k.lower()] = title

broken_render = []
for p in pathlib.Path(&#39;content/english/notes&#39;).glob(&#39;*.md&#39;):
    raw = p.read_text().split(&#39;---&#39;,2)[-1]
    # strip fences and inline code like hugo
    raw = re.sub(r&#39;\`\`\`[\s\S]*?\`\`\`&#39;, &#39;&#39;, raw)
    raw = re.sub(r&#39;\`[^\`\n]+\`&#39;, &#39;&#39;, raw)
    for m in re.finditer(r&#39;\[\[([^\]|#]+)(?:#[^\]|]+)?(?:\|[^\]]+)?\]\]&#39;, raw):
        target = m.group(1).strip()
        if target.lower() not in registry:
            broken_render.append((p.name, target))

print(&#39;Registry miss (lint should catch):&#39;, len(broken_render))
for x in broken_render[:20]: print(x)
&#34;</summary>
```bash
cd /home/jorap/Documents/GitHub/jorap.com && python3 -c "
import re, pathlib
registry = {}
for p in pathlib.Path('content/english/notes').glob('*.md'):
    text = p.read_text()
    m = re.search(r'^title:\s*\"([^\"]+)\"', text, re.M)
    if not m: continue
    title = m.group(1)
    slug = re.search(r'^slug:\s*\"([^\"]+)\"', text, re.M)
    slug = slug.group(1) if slug else p.stem
    aliases = re.findall(r'aliases:\s*\[([^\]]+)\]', text, re.M)
    keys = [title, slug, p.stem]
    for a in aliases:
        keys += [x.strip().strip('\"') for x in a.group(1).split(',')]
    for k in keys:
        registry[k.lower()] = title

broken_render = []
for p in pathlib.Path('content/english/notes').glob('*.md'):
    raw = p.read_text().split('---',2)[-1]
    # strip fences and inline code like hugo
    raw = re.sub(r'\`\`\`[\s\S]*?\`\`\`', '', raw)
    raw = re.sub(r'\`[^\`\n]+\`', '', raw)
    for m in re.finditer(r'\[\[([^\]|#]+)(?:#[^\]|]+)?(?:\|[^\]]+)?\]\]', raw):
        target = m.group(1).strip()
        if target.lower() not in registry:
            broken_render.append((p.name, target))

print('Registry miss (lint should catch):', len(broken_render))
for x in broken_render[:20]: print(x)
"
```

The wiki holds understanding; [[Spaced Repetition Systems (SRS)]] holds drill. Each spine note carries six cards in frontmatter; opt in sparingly. Pairs with [[PKM]], [[Pareto Principle]], and [[RSS for Research]].

Replacing matches one by one is unsafe because each replacement shifts content indices. The revised approach is to loop over exact full-match strings instead of using findRESubmatch.

The template draft now resolves each wikilink via partial and replaces it with an anchor tag. A concern arises that Hugo's `replace` function replaces all occurrences, so duplicate links would be handled incorrectly. The template is being rewritten to resolve wikilinks through a single registry pass instead of per-note regex keys. This handles case sensitivity, parentheses, and aliases uniformly. The new render-content.html normalizes typographer quotes and protects code blocks before resolving links.

The template now extracts each wikilink with `findRE`, strips the brackets, splits on pipes for labels, and splits on hashes for anchors.

The template now resolves each target via partial and builds the anchor href with any hash anchor. It sets the link text to the custom label or falls back to the resolved page title. A rule for alias links without custom labels is being added.

The template draft is being finalized. It builds an anchor tag and replaces the full match in the content string. A critical flaw was spotted: the loop iterates over the original `findRE` matches, but each replacement shifts the content indices, so later matches may be missed.

A dictionary will track seen wikilinks to prevent double-processing during replacement. Re-scanning the content after each replacement was considered but rejected. Matches inside anchor attributes are unlikely.

The display text for wikilinks without custom labels needs to be the original target string, not the resolved page title. This preserves alias names like "PARA" while still showing full titles for longer targets.

The parser is being refined to support full wikilink syntax including anchors and labels. Anchor-only links like `[[Title#heading]]` require special handling.

The parser must split on the pipe first, then on the hash. Anchor-only links like `[[Title#heading]]` should display the heading text as the link label.

_**Agent (default)**_

The “broken” links you were seeing were mostly **false alarms** — the issues page was right that they resolve in the registry; rendering was wrong.

**What was going wrong**

The issues linter scans raw markdown and looks up targets case-insensitively in the link registry. Wikilink rendering used a different path: exact-string replacement on Hugo’s processed HTML. That failed when:

1. **Typographer** curled apostrophes inside `[[...]]` (e.g. `Collector's` → `Collector's`)
2. **Case differed** from the stored title (e.g. `[[Cards]]` vs slug `cards` / title `Flashcards`)
3. **Titles had parentheses** (e.g. `[[Spaced Repetition Systems (SRS)]]` — regex escaping broke)
4. **Self-links** were skipped during replacement, then marked broken
5. **Code examples** like `` `[[wikilinks]]` `` were converted to broken spans (the linter correctly ignores inline code)

So notes looked broken while `/notes/issues/` said “all clear.”

**What I fixed**

- Rewrote `render-content.html` to resolve each `[[wikilink]]` through the same `resolve-target` / registry lookup the linter uses
- Protected `<code>` and `<pre>` blocks from wikilink substitution
- Normalized typographer apostrophes before resolving
- Fixed duplicate/broken `[[Cards]]` line in `review.md` → `[[Flashcards]]`
- Busted the issues cache key to `note-issues-v2`

After rebuild: **zero** `wikilink-broken` spans across the garden, and the issues page still reports no missing notes — which is now consistent.

If you add a wikilink that truly doesn’t match any title, slug, or alias, it should show as broken on the note **and** appear on `/notes/issues/`. Rebuild the site to pick up the template changes.

---

_**User**_

ensure that the following are not considered as notes:
Graph view
Issues
Flashcards
Review
Random note
2 random notes
3 random notes

---

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
Garden-pages already excludes meta.

random-pool incorrectly pulls from pages.html, which includes meta pages and breaks random note selection. It should use garden-pages instead. Other modules like link-registry, backlinks, related-notes, and collect-cards also source from pages.html, so meta pages may leak into those features too.

Preview-index also leaks meta pages into previews through link-registry. The cleanest fix is to centralize exclusion logic in `pages.html` by filtering out meta notes and utility layouts. `garden-pages.html` would then serve as the canonical note list. Whether `link-registry.html` should still pull from `garden-pages` or keep its own meta-inclusive path for wikilink resolution remains undecided.

Utility pages must be excluded from the note grid, graph nodes, recent updates, and total counts. Wikilinks should still resolve to them. Meta pages are currently leaking into random pools, backlinks, and related notes.

related-notes, backlinks, and collect-cards should all use garden-pages instead of pages.html. Whether link-registry should remain meta-inclusive is still unresolved; removing meta pages from the registry would break wikilinks to utility pages like Graph and Flashcards.

`pages.html` currently excludes only random-two and random-three layouts, leaving graph pages in the note list. A dedicated `link-registry-pages.html` partial or an `IncludeUtility` flag would let the registry keep meta pages while other views filter them out.

`pages.html` only excludes meta pages when its caller explicitly passes `ExcludeKinds: meta`. `garden-pages.html` does this, but `random-pool.html` does not, so meta pages leak into the random pool. `pages.html` also omits graph, cards, review, and issues from its layout exclusion list.

`pages.html` currently includes all pages when called with an empty dictionary, so both random-pool and link-registry leak meta pages. The cleanest fix is to default `pages.html` to exclude utility layouts and meta pages, then add an `IncludeUtility` flag for link-registry. Another option is to split the partials entirely: `garden-pages.html` for real notes only, and `pages.html` for the registry.
</details></think>

<summary>Tool use: **run_terminal_command_v2** • Run command: cd /home/jorap/Documents/GitHub/jorap.com &amp;&amp; python3 -c &#34;
import json, re
html = open(&#39;public/notes/index.html&#39;).read()
m = re.search(r&#39;class=notes-random-data&gt;(\[.*?\])&lt;/script&gt;&#39;, html)
pool = json.loads(m.group(1))
titles = {x[&#39;title&#39;] for x in pool}
utility = {&#39;Graph&#39;,&#39;Issues&#39;,&#39;Flashcards&#39;,&#39;Review&#39;,&#39;2 random notes&#39;,&#39;3 random notes&#39;,&#39;Random note&#39;}
found = utility &amp; titles
print(&#39;pool size:&#39;, len(pool))
print(&#39;utility in pool:&#39;, found or &#39;none&#39;)
for t in [&#39;Graph&#39;,&#39;Issues&#39;,&#39;Flashcards&#39;,&#39;Review&#39;,&#39;Getting Started&#39;,&#39;PKM&#39;]:
    print(t, &#39;in pool:&#39;, t in titles)
&#34;</summary>
```bash
cd /home/jorap/Documents/GitHub/jorap.com && python3 -c "
import json, re
html = open('public/notes/index.html').read()
m = re.search(r'class=notes-random-data>(\[.*?\])</script>', html)
pool = json.loads(m.group(1))
titles = {x['title'] for x in pool}
utility = {'Graph','Issues','Flashcards','Review','2 random notes','3 random notes','Random note'}
found = utility & titles
print('pool size:', len(pool))
print('utility in pool:', found or 'none')
for t in ['Graph','Issues','Flashcards','Review','Getting Started','PKM']:
    print(t, 'in pool:', t in titles)
"
```

```
pool size: 80
utility in pool: none
Graph in pool: False
Issues in pool: False
Flashcards in pool: False
Review in pool: False
Getting Started in pool: True
PKM in pool: True

```
</details></tool-use>

```bash
cd /home/jorap/Documents/GitHub/jorap.com && python3 -c "
import json, re
html = open('public/notes/graph/index.html').read()
m = re.search(r'class=notes-graph-data>(\{.*?\})</script>', html)
g = json.loads(m.group(1))
titles = {n['title'] for n in g['nodes']}
utility = {'Graph','Issues','Flashcards','Review'}
print('graph nodes:', len(titles))
print('utility in graph:', utility & titles or 'none')
"
```

_**Agent (default)**_

Utility pages are now excluded from “garden notes” everywhere that matters.

**New partial:** `is-utility-page.html` — marks a page as utility when it has `note_kind: meta` or layout is `graph`, `cards`, `review`, `issues`, `random-two`, or `random-three`.

**`pages.html`** — by default returns only real garden notes (utility excluded). Pass `IncludeUtility: true` when you need utility pages in the set (wikilink registry only).
